
# Telecom Customer Churn Prediction

### Machine Learning Project — CRISP-DM Framework

**Author:** Richard Oketch  
**Dataset:** Telecom Customer Churn Dataset (3,333 records, 21 features)  
**Target Variable:** `churn` (Boolean — True if customer cancelled service)

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Overview](#1-overview)
2. [Business and Data Understanding](#2-business-and-data-understanding)
3. [Modeling](#3-modeling)
4. [Evaluation](#4-evaluation)
5. [Conclusion](#5-conclusion)
6. [Repository Structure](#6-repository-structure)
7. [Contributors](#7-contributors)

---

## 1. Overview

Customer churn — when a customer stops doing business with a company — is one of the most expensive problems in telecommunications. Acquiring a new customer costs **five to seven times more** than retaining an existing one. For a telecom provider managing thousands of accounts, even a 1% improvement in retention translates to measurable recurring revenue.

This project builds a machine learning model to predict which customers are likely to churn *before* they decide to leave, giving the retention team a prioritised call list to intervene with targeted offers and service improvements.

**The core deliverable is not just a model — it is a weekly scoring pipeline that ranks every active customer by their churn probability, enabling proactive outreach rather than reactive win-back campaigns.**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Customers analysed | 3,333 |
| Annual churn rate | 14.49% (~483 customers/year) |
| Models trained & compared | 5 |
| Final model ROC-AUC | **0.882** |
| Churners caught (Recall) | **72.2%** |

---

## 2. Business and Data Understanding

### 2.1 Stakeholder Audience

The primary audience is the **Customer Retention & Marketing team** at a mid-sized US telecom operator. This team is responsible for proactively reaching out to at-risk customers with targeted offers, service upgrades, or loyalty incentives *before* those customers cancel.

The project delivers two things they can act on immediately:
1. A **priority-ranked list** of customers by churn probability, exported to their CRM weekly.
2. Three **specific business rules** (auto-flag triggers) derived from the model's top features — no model required to apply them.

A secondary audience is **senior leadership**, who need to understand the business case: what the model can do, what it cannot yet do, and what investment is required to run it in production.

### 2.2 Dataset Choice

The dataset contains **3,333 customer records** with **21 features** covering usage patterns, plan subscriptions, account details, and service call history. It was selected because:

- It contains features a retention team can actually act on (service calls, plan type, usage level) — not just demographic proxies.
- The 14.49% churn rate creates a realistic class imbalance problem, making it a meaningful testbed for recall-optimised modelling.
- Zero missing values means the full analytical effort can focus on modelling rather than imputation.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Rows | 3,333 |
| Features | 21 (13 used after preparation) |
| Churned customers | 483 (14.49%) |
| Retained customers | 2,850 (85.51%) |
| Missing values | 0 |

### 2.3 Dataset Structure & Quality Audit

| Column | Type | Issue | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| `phone number` | object | Unique identifier, no signal | Dropped |
| `state` | object | 51 categories — high cardinality | Dropped |
| `area code` | int | Only 3 unique values, no signal | Dropped |
| `total day charge` | float | r = 1.00 with `total day minutes` | Dropped (collinear) |
| `total eve charge` | float | r = 1.00 with `total eve minutes` | Dropped (collinear) |
| `total night charge` | float | r = 1.00 with `total night minutes` | Dropped (collinear) |
| `total intl charge` | float | r = 1.00 with `total intl minutes` | Dropped (collinear) |
| `international plan` | object | yes/no binary | Encoded → 1/0 |
| `voice mail plan` | object | yes/no binary | Encoded → 1/0 |
| `churn` | bool | Target variable | Encoded → 1/0 |

The correlation heatmap confirmed that all four charge columns are perfect linear multiples of their corresponding minute columns — retaining both would add zero information while inflating model complexity.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Correlation Heatmap" width="100%"/>

*Correlation heatmap across all numeric features. The four charge–minute pairs show r = 1.00 — one of each pair was dropped before modelling.*

### 2.4 Key EDA Findings

#### Churn Rate — Class Imbalance

At 14.49%, the dataset is moderately imbalanced. A naive model that predicts "no churn" for every customer achieves 85.5% accuracy but catches **zero churners**. This makes accuracy a misleading metric — the analysis uses **Recall** and **F1-score** instead.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Waffle Chart — Churn Rate" width="80%"/>

*Each square represents 1% of customers. 14 red squares = 483 churned customers. The waffle format makes the class imbalance more intuitive than a pie chart.*

#### Usage Distributions — Churned vs Retained

KDE plots reveal how feature distributions differ between churned and retained customers. The key finding: **day minutes is the clearest separator**, with churned customers averaging 207 minutes vs 175 for retained — higher usage drives higher bills, which drives price sensitivity. Night and evening minutes show nearly identical distributions between the two groups, confirming they are weaker predictors.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="KDE Usage Distributions" width="100%"/>

*KDE plots for six usage features split by churn status. The separation in Total Day Minutes (top-left) is visibly larger than all other panels — confirming its role as the top predictive feature.*

#### Violin Plots — Distribution Shape and Spread

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Violin Plots" width="100%"/>

*Day minutes shows a clear rightward shift for churned customers. Eve, Night, and Intl minutes show largely overlapping distributions — the difference is much smaller and less actionable.*

#### Customer Service Calls — The Clearest Threshold Signal

The most actionable finding in the entire dataset: churn rate is approximately **11% for customers with 0–3 service calls**, but jumps to **51.7% at 4+ calls** — a 4.6× increase. This is a **threshold effect** that can be operationalised as a direct business rule without any model.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Strip Plot — Service Calls" width="100%"/>

*Top panel: individual customer dots by service call count and churn status. Bottom panel: churn rate line overlay. The sharp jump at 4 calls is unmistakable — this threshold becomes Recommendation 1.*

#### Pair Plot — Top Feature Interactions

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Pair Plot" width="100%"/>

*Pairwise scatter plots for the top four predictive features coloured by churn status. Day minutes separates the classes along the x-axis across almost every panel. Customers with many voicemail messages (bottom-right panels) rarely churn.*

#### Data Understanding Summary

| Finding | Implication |
|---|---|
| 14.49% churn rate | Imbalanced — use Recall & F1, not accuracy |
| International plan: 42.4% vs 11.5% churn | Strongest single binary predictor |
| 4+ customer service calls: ~52% churn | Non-linear threshold effect — directly actionable |
| Churned avg 207 vs 175 day minutes | Higher usage → higher bills → price sensitivity |
| Voice mail plan: 8.7% vs 16.7% churn | Protective factor and retention lever |
| Charge = 1.00 × minutes | Drop charge columns — perfect collinearity |
| Zero missing values | No imputation required |

---

## 3. Modeling

### 3.1 Data Preparation

After the quality audit, the final model-ready dataset was constructed as follows:

- **Columns dropped:** `phone number`, `state`, `area code`, and all four charge columns (7 dropped total → 13 features + 1 target)
- **Binary encoding:** `international plan` and `voice mail plan` → 1/0; `churn` target → 1/0
- **Train/test split:** 80/20 stratified — 2,666 training rows and 667 test rows, both at ~14.5% churn rate
- **Feature scaling:** `StandardScaler` fitted on training data only, applied to both sets for Logistic Regression models. Tree-based models use raw features.
- **Final shape:** 3,333 rows × 14 columns, 0 missing values

### 3.2 Model Strategy

Five models were trained in sequence, each addressing a limitation of the previous:

| Model | Type | Key Change | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | Logistic Regression (Baseline) | Default settings | Establish performance floor; expose class imbalance impact |
| M2 | Logistic Regression (Tuned) | `class_weight='balanced'` + GridSearch on C | Correct for imbalance; find optimal regularisation |
| M3 | Decision Tree (Baseline) | Default settings | Test non-linear patterns; churn threshold effects are non-linear |
| M4 | Decision Tree (Tuned) | GridSearch on depth, leaf size, class weight | Reduce overfitting; constrain the tree |
| **M5** | **Random Forest (Final)** | **200 trees, bagging, random feature subsets** | **Best generalisation — ensemble variance reduction** |

### 3.3 Model Results — Overfitting Diagnostic

The dumbbell chart below shows the train vs test recall gap for every model. A long bar = overfitting. M3 (Decision Tree Baseline) shows textbook overfitting (train=1.00, test=0.649, gap=0.351). M5 (Random Forest) achieves the smallest gap of all models — it generalises most reliably to unseen data.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Dumbbell Chart — Train vs Test Recall Gap" width="100%"/>

| Model | Train Recall | Test Recall | Gap | Test F1 | Test AUC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1: LR Baseline | 0.207 | 0.247 | 0.040 | 0.321 | 0.817 |
| M2: LR Tuned | ~0.764 | 0.742 | 0.022 | 0.474 | 0.816 |
| M3: DT Baseline | 1.000 | 0.649 | **0.351** | 0.663 | 0.798 |
| M4: DT Tuned | 0.938 | 0.742 | 0.196 | 0.673 | 0.833 |
| **M5: RF Final** | **0.852** | **0.722** | **0.130** | **0.622** | **0.882** |

### 3.4 Radar Chart — All Metrics at Once

The radar chart compares all five models across Recall, F1, AUC, and Precision simultaneously. The Random Forest (bold purple) presents the most balanced profile — strong across all four metrics rather than dominating on one at the expense of others.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Radar Chart — All Models" width="80%"/>

### 3.5 Confusion Matrices — Model-by-Model

The confusion matrices track how each model's false negative count (churners missed — top-right cell) evolves across the sequence. Reducing false negatives is the primary goal.

| M1: LR Baseline | M2: LR Tuned |
|---|---|
| <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="M1" width="100%"/> | <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="M2" width="100%"/> |

| M3: DT Baseline | M4: DT Tuned |
|---|---|
| <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="M3" width="100%"/> | <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="M4" width="100%"/> |

**M5: Random Forest (Final)**

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="M5 — Final Model" width="50%"/>

### 3.6 Tuned Decision Tree — Top 3 Levels

The decision tree diagram confirms that the model's first splits are on `total day minutes` then `customer service calls` — exactly the two features EDA identified as the strongest predictors. The model is not finding spurious patterns; it is capturing what the data shows.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Tuned Decision Tree" width="100%"/>

---

## 4. Evaluation

### 4.1 Why These Metrics

**Accuracy is not used** as the primary metric. A model predicting "no churn" for every customer achieves 85.5% accuracy but catches zero churners. For an imbalanced problem where the cost of a miss is high, **Recall** is the primary business metric.

| Metric | Business Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Recall** | Of every 100 customers who will churn, how many do we flag? |
| **Precision** | Of every 100 customers we flag, how many are genuine risks? |
| **F1-Score** | Harmonic mean — balances precision and recall |
| **ROC-AUC** | Probability the model ranks a churner higher than a non-churner — measures ranking quality at every threshold |

### 4.2 ROC Curves — All Models

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="ROC Curves" width="100%"/>

*M5 (solid purple, AUC=0.882) clearly outperforms all others. M1 (dashed blue, AUC=0.817) shows that logistic regression always had ranking ability — the class imbalance correction in M2, and the ensemble in M5, unlocked that potential.*

### 4.3 Precision-Recall Curve — Final Model

For imbalanced datasets, the PR curve is more informative than the ROC curve. It shows every recall level's achievable precision — the retention team can choose where on this curve to operate based on their outreach capacity.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Precision-Recall Curve" width="90%"/>

*The colour gradient shows how the decision threshold changes along the curve. The blue star marks the default 0.50 threshold. Lowering the threshold moves right (more recall, lower precision). The team should tune this based on how many calls they can handle per week.*

### 4.4 Final Model Performance

**Model:** Random Forest (n_estimators=200, max_depth=5, class_weight=balanced)  
**Test set:** 667 customers — 97 churned, 570 retained

| Result | Count | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| True Positives (churners caught) | **70** | Customers the team can proactively contact |
| False Negatives (churners missed) | 27 | Customers lost despite the model |
| False Positives (non-churners flagged) | 58 | Wasted outreach — budgeted for |
| True Negatives (correctly cleared) | 512 | No action needed |

| Metric | Score |
|---|---|
| Recall | **0.722** — 70 of 97 churners identified |
| Precision | 0.547 — 55% of flagged customers are genuine |
| F1-Score | 0.622 |
| ROC-AUC | **0.882** |

### 4.5 Feature Importance

**Lollipop chart — ranked feature importances:**

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Feature Importance Lollipop" width="100%"/>

*All 13 features ranked by importance. The dashed navy line marks the 70th percentile threshold. Features above it (coral) are top-tier predictors. Total day minutes and customer service calls dominate.*

**Treemap — proportional importance:**

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Feature Importance Treemap" width="100%"/>

*Rectangle area is proportional to importance. Total day minutes (0.303) and customer service calls (0.229) together account for over 50% of the model's decision-making.*

| Rank | Feature | Importance | Business Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `total day minutes` | **0.303** | Heavy users face higher bills → price sensitivity → churn |
| 2 | `customer service calls` | **0.229** | 4+ calls = deep dissatisfaction → escalate immediately |
| 3 | `international plan` | **0.154** | 42.4% vs 11.5% churn — plan may be poor value |
| 4 | `total eve minutes` | 0.062 | Secondary usage signal |
| 5 | `number vmail messages` | 0.058 | Protective factor — voicemail users rarely churn |
| 6 | `total intl minutes` | 0.047 | High intl usage + intl plan = compounded risk |
| 7–13 | Remaining features | < 0.04 each | Supporting signals; limited individual impact |

### 4.6 Model Selection Justification

M4 (Decision Tree Tuned) and M5 (Random Forest) are close on some metrics. The table below explains why M5 was selected:

| Criterion | M4: Decision Tree | M5: Random Forest | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test Recall | 0.742 | 0.722 | Within noise — tie |
| Test F1 | 0.673 | 0.622 | DT slightly higher |
| **Test AUC** | 0.833 | **0.882** | **RF significantly higher** |
| **Train-test gap** | 0.196 | **0.130** | **RF generalises better** |

M5 was selected for two decisive reasons:

1. **ROC-AUC 0.882 vs 0.833.** The retention team scores customers by probability, not binary flag. AUC directly measures ranking quality at every threshold — a 5-point gap is material in production.
2. **Smallest generalisation gap (0.130).** M4's gap of 0.196 suggests partial memorisation of training data. M5's smaller gap means its performance on future unseen customers will be more predictable and reliable.

---

## 5. Conclusion

### 5.1 Key Findings

| Rank | Feature | Key Stat | Business Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total day minutes** | Churners avg 207 vs 175 min | Heavy users face higher bills → offer loyalty rate cap |
| 2 | **Customer service calls** | 52% churn at 4+ calls vs 11% at 0–3 | Threshold effect → auto-escalate after 3rd call |
| 3 | **International plan** | 42.4% churn vs 11.5% without | Plan may be poor value → audit and exit interviews |
| 4 | **Total eve minutes** | Secondary usage signal | Monitor alongside day minutes |
| 5 | **Total intl minutes** | High intl usage + intl plan = compounded risk | Offer discounted intl bundles |

**Protective factor:** Voice mail plan holders churn at **8.7% vs 16.7%** — offer it free as a retention incentive.

### 5.2 Business Recommendations

**Recommendation 1 — Auto-Flag After the 3rd Service Call (Immediate)**  
Churn jumps from ~11% to ~52% at 4+ calls. Route any customer logging a 3rd call to a senior retention agent before the 4th call occurs. This is a business rule — no model required to deploy it today.

**Recommendation 2 — Rate Cap Loyalty Offer for Heavy Daytime Users (Within 30 Days)**  
Customers above the 75th percentile in daytime usage (> 216 min/day) are simultaneously your highest-value and highest-risk segment. Proactively offering a price guarantee removes the primary financial stressor driving their departure.

**Recommendation 3 — Audit the International Plan + Offer Voicemail as Incentive (Within 60 Days)**  
42.4% of international plan holders churn — nearly 4× the base rate. Benchmark pricing against competitors and conduct exit interviews. Separately, offer the voicemail plan as a free trial to at-risk segments: holders churn at half the rate of non-holders.

### 5.3 Model Limitations

1. **27.8% of churners are still missed** — recall of 72.2% means 1 in 4 churners are not flagged. The threshold can be lowered to catch more, but this increases false positives and outreach cost.
2. **No demographic or contract data** — age, income, and contract type are known churn drivers but absent from this dataset. Adding them is the highest-value next step.
3. **No timestamps — static snapshot** — the model cannot detect seasonal patterns or model churn as a time-to-event problem. A survival model on longitudinal data would outperform this approach.
4. **Static model** — must be retrained as customer behaviour and competitive dynamics change. Quarterly retraining recommended.
5. **45% false positive rate among flagged customers** — every outreach costs money. Budget approximately 0.8 non-churners contacted per genuine churner flagged.

### 5.4 Deployment Plan

| Phase | Timeline | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 | Weeks 1–2 | Deploy weekly scoring pipeline to CRM; export priority-ranked at-risk customer list; set operating threshold with retention team |
| Phase 2 | Month 1–2 | Run A/B test — hold out a random control group of high-risk customers; measure revenue retained vs outreach cost; tune threshold based on team capacity |
| Phase 3 | Quarterly | Retrain on newest data; add contract type and demographic features; target: push recall above 80% with richer data |

---

## 6. Repository Structure

```
├── churn_telecoms_dataset.csv              # Raw dataset
├── churn_telecoms_cleaned.csv              # Model-ready dataset (post-preparation)
├── telecom_churn_analysis_notebook.ipynb  # Full analysis notebook
├── README.md                              # This file — all visuals embedded inline
├── presentation.pdf                       # Non-technical slide deck
└── .gitignore
```

**Tools used:**

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Python 3 | Core language |
| pandas / NumPy | Data manipulation |
| Matplotlib / Seaborn | Visualisation |
| scikit-learn | Modelling, evaluation, GridSearchCV |
| squarify | Treemap visualisation |

*This README is the analytical bridge between the non-technical presentation slides and the full Jupyter Notebook. It does not reproduce code but provides the methodology, findings, and business context in depth. For implementation details, see `telecom_churn_analysis_notebook.ipynb`.*
